With Arlyeon's authorization, we will be releasing the text version in four parts on the "Morimens" Discord channel. For the full video version, please click the link below to watch~

embeds.title: Surviving Oblivion - Talking Morimens W/ Light & Jiyue Of Brain In ...
embeds.description:
So, after many a year, I finally dipped my toes into #morimens - a #lovecraftianhorror fueled #roguelite #deckbuilder - and in doing so, wound up with a *lot* of questions.

Questions I was fortunately able to get answered, given that their Studio is fairly friendly. So, join me as I find out more a

content: first

> ***Part IV 关于《忘却前夜》的抽卡机制和对社区生态***

> ***Q46***
> 首先，与同品类许多制作组相比，你和社区互动非常积极。在培养活跃、健康的玩家社区方面，你们遇到的最大困难是什么？最有成就感的部分又是什么？

***A46***
我选择与社区保持互动的原因是多方面的，而且也在不断演变。

从 2021 年底几乎推翻原方案、重做 DEMO，到 2023 年末正式上线《忘却前夜》，我们在不到两年的时间里完成了这一切。那段时间团队必须尽快做判断、尽快执行，并尽可能多地吸收玩家反馈；但现实是预算有限，时间与验证周期也极其紧张。我很清楚，在这种条件下推出的游戏一定会有不少问题，而玩家对同品类头部游戏的预期又很高，中间难免产生落差。这样的处境也很难“伪装”过去，所以我选择把情况尽量讲清楚，用坦诚去争取理解与支持。

游戏的玩法与系统复杂度决定了：游戏设计师不可能在研发阶段穷尽所有问题。为了更快捕捉玩家在实际游玩中的体验反馈，并用它来指导优化、弥补运营人手不足的窘境，我必须在社区里持续沟通，尽自己所能记录问题、推进解决。再加上我并非这个项目最初的发起者，游戏的核心方向也比较新，当时市面上很难找到一款受众与《忘却前夜》完全重合的“参照物”，传统市场调研也很难直接给出答案；因此我需要亲自到社区里去看：大家如何理解这款游戏、遇到什么问题、真正需要什么。

从个人角度来说，能把团队的创作成果平稳交到大家手里，并持续听到大家的声音、把游戏越做越好，这件事本身就让我很开心。然而我也很清楚，随着这种沟通方式逐渐得到了更多人的认可，最后当我因为其他事务减少露面时，大家也会表达不满——从那时起，持续坦诚沟通就不只是我的理性选择或个人偏好，也成了这个游戏社区生态、甚至是品牌形象的一部分。我深知将个人形象与作品绑定会带来极大的风险，团队内部也一度对此存在不少争议。

而最大的困难，也恰恰是这一点。在游戏的第一个年头，我和大家互动得太松弛、太随性，忽略了这个问题。这在风平浪静时问题不明显，但当我们后来不得不推进一些与商业化、游玩压力相关的调整，或在平衡上出现失误、需要回滚或修正时，过去一些不够周全的表达就容易被集中放大。而在游戏运营的第一年末，我长期处于身心透支状态，这让我在面对一些极端、伤人的言论时，情绪更容易受到影响，而进一步犯下更多错误。

在最脆弱的时刻，我甚至一度想到要引咎辞职，但我很清楚自己背后还没有人可以接下这个担子。后来经过痛苦的反思后，我发觉最好的赎罪不是辞职，而是尽自己所能将游戏发展得更好！后来我在社区沟通时依然保持坦诚，但也会更注意身份与分寸，选择更合适的渠道进行沟通，也能更加冷静地看待不同声音。于是第二年开始，类似的失误少了很多。

最让我有成就感的，是当有玩家告诉我，《忘却前夜》中某个角色的故事帮助他们度过了自己生活中的某个困难时刻。这就是我最初被打动选择投身于游戏行业的契机，此刻一切经历的困难与痛苦都变成了真正的回报……

> ***Q47***
> 你们是否计划在游戏之外与社区进行更多互动？例如举办绘画比赛、写作比赛等活动？

***A47***
官方 Discord 社区中会不定期举办同人创作类活动，后续也会继续推进，并尝试更多形式的社区共创内容。欢迎大家多多关注、积极参与！

> ***Q48***
> 顺着这个问题，你们是否计划直接与内容创作者合作？无论是主播还是视频创作者。

***A48***
首先我想特别感谢 THPDom 创作的《Why I fell in love with this card-based gacha game》。他在视频里提到，《忘却前夜》在他面对死亡的概念时给予了更多勇气——这与我们想表达的主题高度契合，那一刻我真的被感动到流泪。

也正是因为他在视频中用语音完整朗读了终章最后一节的剧情，我才下定决心为那段内容追加配音预算。他的作品也为《忘却前夜》带来了许多忠实的新玩家。

从那之后，我就开始认真思考：或许应该与更多内容创作者展开合作，来继续壮大社区。目前我们还在探索更合适、可持续的合作方式进行。

> ***Q49***
> 之前有不少暗示说游戏未来可能会有联动。能不能稍微提示一下，联动会是角色联动，还是纯剧情活动？是双向联动还是单向联动？

***A49***
联动的表现形式不限：既可能有像《逆转回合》那样的纯剧情活动，也可能有像《雾境序列》那样的角色单向联动。未来当然也不排除双向联动。

在联动合作的周期上，我们会坚持“不做一次性内容”的原则：联动相关内容会尽可能以“未来仍有机会再次获取”的形式呈现，而不是错过就永远无法获得。毕竟《忘却前夜》的主题是“以记忆对抗融蚀”，如果联动内容无法以某种方式长期留在游戏里，确实会与《忘却前夜》的叙事精神相悖。

当然，基于行业与授权的现实规律，通常只能签署数年期限内不限复刻次数、到期后再续约的合作授权协议；如果未来出现授权到期且因客观原因无法续约的情况，我们会提前告知大家，并做好相应的安排与预案。

至于最近即将推出的联动：它会是单向联动，对方是一部影响深远、带有宇宙恐怖风味的经典作品。这将是一次特别的角色联动。

> ***Q50***
> 接下来是一个比较尖锐的问题。正如我在开篇所说，《忘却前夜》属于抽卡游戏，而这个品类以与玩家形成掠夺式消费关系而臭名昭著。但到目前为止，我觉得这款游戏对玩家相当慷慨。
> 你们采取了哪些措施避免这类负面关系，规避抽卡游戏最受诟病的问题？你对目前的商业化模式满意吗？有没有想落地的、对玩家更友好的措施？

***A50***

抽卡模式确实有被滥用的历史，我无意为这种模式本身辩护。我只能解释B.I.A.V.工作室在这一框架内做出的具体选择，以及这样做的理由。

基于商业现实、团队想要实现的工作规模以及瞄准移动市场的选择，第一家制作公司在2019年开发之初就选择了抽卡模式。然而，自2023年我们进行测试和发布以来，系统设计师们一直在努力“消除”这一模式中最有可能引发冲突和焦虑的部分，使其更加透明、克制和可持续。在掉落率、保底规则、礼包价值和游戏内奖励分配方面，我们试图比同类型的其他游戏更加友好。

游戏发布时，还提供了每月可兑换的通用特殊材料，这些材料可以用来增强角色，而不是重复抽取——这意味着愿意投入时间的玩家可以逐渐将他们喜爱的角色发展到更高的完成度。我们甚至在第一年减少了硬保底次数，使每个人都能更容易地获得角色。在游戏玩法方面，游戏还提供了免费的自动恢复复活道具，并且在第一年我们取消了普通模式中禁止使用应急灵知体的成就要求，使玩家能够更自由地使用这些机制来减轻压力。在奖励设计方面，我们也努力将“错过就永远错过”的焦虑感降至最低：即使是限时奖励，通常在一段时间后也会通过其他方式再次获得。

然而，我们并不认为当前的情况是完美的。早期，为了保留货币化空间，启灵和命轮的设计采用了相对较高的重复拉取要求，同时将关键的机制优势集中在启灵的前三个等级或叠位上。虽然这对大多数玩家来说是有利的，但也让少数希望专注于特定角色的玩家感到他们的投入很高，但获得的回报感却不足。数据还显示，总体支付意愿低于预期，而“满级所需重复次数过高”的反馈相当集中。面对实际的商业和运营需求，游戏后来不得不逐步引入过度提升、额外等级上限和最终公理——这些内容将在未来继续逐步增加。虽然这些对现有进度线的进一步增强确实带来了货币化的改进，但也引发了争议，并为玩家带来了一定的心理负担。

在面对需要维持商业运营与不想给玩家施加过多压力之间的矛盾时，由于经验和理解不足，我们的处理方式确实欠佳。如果能够重来一次，我们会在上线前更深入地权衡这些利弊：适当减少重复要求的数量，并在掉落率和保底机制上寻求更合理的平衡，以减少因反复进行运营中期调整而带来的意外焦虑。回到现在——由于我们必须谨慎地对货币化和进度系统进行核心结构调整，我们计划未来的方向是：在每个主要版本中增加免费福利和活动奖励；提供更多机会获取之前有限的内容；推出更多便捷、个性化和减轻压力的功能；并继续优化重复角色的溢出奖励。
我们一直希望通过不断迭代，游戏整体能越来越对玩家友好。

> ***Q51***
> 插个小问题：错失焦虑（FOMO）是很多抽卡游戏的核心设计，但到目前为止，我还没在游戏里遇到和核心内容绑定的真正限时绝版内容——没有真正限定角色或命轮，即使联动角色也是如此。不过限时的回响之途礼包仍让我有些不适。这个商业化设计会一直保留吗？还是会更多推出像盘龙礼包那样有效期更长的活动礼包，或是放进“纪事”礼包？那些没赶上的回响之途礼包，有没有可能重新开放，缓解玩家的错失焦虑？

***A51***
错失焦虑确实是极为有效的设计手段：无论是有用的玩法内容、好看的外观，还是很普通的奖励，只要被标记为“绝版”，就会显著提高玩家的获取冲动。所有团队成员同样也是玩家，因为开发工作很忙，也未必总能及时去玩自己喜欢的游戏，和大家一样深受绝版焦虑之苦。

不过《忘却前夜》也难以完全免俗——游戏里仍然会有一些“限时”的内容，比如限时卡池、赛季纪念品、活动特有命轮、活动产出的幻衣等。但正如你观察到的，游戏内几乎没有“真正绝版”的核心内容：这类限时内容在一段时间后通常都会复刻，或提供其他再次获取的途径，只是代价可能更高，或需要等待更久。

至于有时间限制的活动礼包，此前确实存在永久错失焦虑问题。但我们已经确定会在未来的更新中提供一种合理间隔、合理条件的方式来重新开启过期未购买的礼包，来进一步将措施焦虑控制在最低限度内。

> ***Q52***
> 说到投入，你曾公开说过，如果《忘却前夜》最终停服，你们一定会做一个单机版本，让大家继续体验故事与玩法。作为重视游戏遗产保护的玩家，我非常欣赏这个决定。这个单机版本会对新玩家开放吗？还是更多回馈老玩家？考虑到现有的抽卡、体力系统，你们计划如何将这些机制适配成单机玩法？在单机版本里，角色和启灵容易获取吗？时装之类内容会保留，还是仅作为老玩家纪念？

***A52***
这个单机版本会尽可能保留玩家的通关进度、账号养成、外观道具、成就、典藏馆等解锁情况；但它并不仅仅是给老玩家做一个“纪念相册”。有些游戏停服后为大家带来了离线版本，这让我深受启发——例如横尾太郎的《死亡爱丽丝》。我们希望在这个基础上更进一步，使之变为一款仍然可以认真游玩、也对新玩家开放的完整作品。

在技术方案上，我们计划在单机版中内置一个“虚拟私人服务端”，用于在本地模拟原本依赖服务器的核心逻辑。单机版会砍掉一些必须在线的功能，并在一定时间内提供账号数据转移到本地客户端的方式。由于转为本地数据，单机版将不再支持多端互通；同时也会支持在本地正常创建新账号来游玩。至于你提到的抽卡、体力获取如何适配、会不会更容易获得等问题：考虑到需要优先尊重大家在网游阶段的历史投入，届时这些设定不会有太大变化。

我知道这个承诺很容易被质疑。由于这一天到来的时机尚早，目前也还没有敲定所有法律和技术细节。但团队已经在做的一件事是：在开发过程中尽量保持架构的可分离性，这样一旦需要，离线版本所需的工程量不会大到无法完成。而我也会在始终保留一笔预算，用于实现这个承诺。这就是目前我能给出的、最诚实的答案。

> ***Q53***
> 最后一个问题：如果《忘却前夜》能一直保持健康热度，你们会尽可能持续扩充世界观吗？还是已经构想好了故事终点？如果有终点，你们后续有项目规划吗？无论是继续做抽卡游戏，还是其他品类。

A53
如果是这样，我们当然会继续扩充世界观！虽然此前第一部终章大幅加快了叙事节奏，也抵达了游戏最初规划的阶段性终点，但我们仍然有很多继续丰富这个世界的想法；目前也暂时没有设定新的故事终点。

如果未来有一天《忘却前夜》发展得足够好，同时团队也确实有全新想探讨的主题、想创造的体验时，我会认真考虑启动后续的新项目——无论是否是抽卡游戏或者是其他品类。

现阶段，我们都希望把尽可能多的精力投入在《忘却前夜》上，努力将它打造成一个能够跨越单个游戏生命周期的长期 IP。这个目标也许很宏大，但 B.I.A.V. Studio 会一步一步把它做下去。

> ***致谢***
> 
> 以上就是我全部的问题。由衷感谢你给我这个机会，深入了解你的想法。能挖掘到这些游戏背后的细节、体验这款游戏，对我来说是一件非常开心的事。也期待未来有机会再次与你交流。

Light：你的问题很深入，很多都触及了《忘却前夜》在开发与运营过程中反复权衡的核心议题。
能通过这次交流回顾团队一路走来的选择与思考，并把未来方向讲得更清楚，对我们而言很有价值。非常感谢你的采访与提问！

> ***Part IV Questions About Morimens by a Gacha / Community Standpoint***

> ***Q46***
> First off — what have been the biggest difficulties insofar as cultivating an active and healthy community — given that you engage with them a lot more actively than a number of your peers which exist in a similar space. And inversely, what have you found to be the most rewarding element?

***A46***
The reasons I choose to engage so closely with the community are multifaceted, and they have continued to evolve over time.

From nearly scrapping our original plans and rebuilding the demo in late 2021 to the official launch of Morimens at the end of 2023, we accomplished all of this in less than two years. During that period, the team had to make judgments as quickly as possible, execute as quickly as possible, and absorb as much player feedback as possible. The reality, however, was that our budget was limited and our time and validation cycles were extremely tight.

 I knew that a game released under those conditions would inevitably have many issues, and because players have high expectations based on the top-tier titles in this genre, a gap between expectation and reality was unavoidable. Our situation was difficult to "mask," so I chose to explain things as clearly as possible and use honesty to earn understanding and support.

The complexity of the game's mechanics and systems also means that game designers cannot possibly exhaust every issue during the development phase. In order to capture player feedback from actual gameplay as quickly as possible and use it to guide improvements — and to make up for the predicament of being short-staffed operationally — I had to maintain constant communication within the community, doing my best to document issues and push for solutions.

 On top of that, since I was not the original initiator of this project and the game's core direction is relatively new, it was difficult at the time to find a "reference point" in the market whose audience perfectly overlapped with Morimens'. Traditional market research couldn't give us direct answers either; so I had to go into the community myself to see how everyone understood the game, what problems they encountered, and what they truly needed.

On a personal level, being able to smoothly deliver the team's creative work into everyone's hands, and to continuously hear their voices and make the game better — that in itself makes me very happy. However, I also recognized that as this mode of communication gradually gained recognition from more and more people, whenever I reduced my appearances due to other responsibilities, people would also express dissatisfaction. 

From that point on, sustained and honest communication was no longer just my rational choice or personal preference — it had also become part of this game's community ecosystem and even its brand identity. I am well aware that binding my personal image to the work carries enormous risks, and there was at one point considerable debate about this within the team.
俺不中嘞沒有對公關人員的感謝嗎比如戈雅無數次幫你貓擦的屁股

And that is precisely where the greatest difficulty lies. During the game's first year, I interacted with the community too loosely and casually, neglecting this issue. 

This wasn't obvious during calm periods, but when we later had to push through adjustments related to monetization and gameplay pressure — or when balance mistakes required rollbacks or corrections — some of my past less-than-thorough expressions were easily amplified and scrutinized all at once. By the end of the game's first year of operation, I had long been in a state of physical and mental exhaustion, which made my emotions more susceptible to being affected when facing extreme or hurtful remarks, leading me to make further mistakes.

In my most vulnerable moments, I even considered resigning out of a sense of responsibility, but I knew there was no one behind me who could take on this responsibility. 

After painful reflection, I realized that the best way to make amends was not to resign, but to do everything in my power to make the game better. Afterwards, I continued to maintain honesty in my community communications, but became more mindful of my role and boundaries, chose more appropriate channels for communication, and was able to view differing opinions with greater calm. As a result, from the second year onward, similar mistakes became far less frequent.

What gives me the greatest sense of achievement is when a player tells me that a character's story in Morimens helped them through a difficult moment in their own life. That was the moment that first moved me to choose a career in the gaming industry — and in those moments, all the hardships and pain I have experienced are transformed into a true reward…

> ***Q47***
> Do you have any plans on further engaging the community outside of the context of the game (such as with art contests, or writing contests)?

***A47***
Fan creation events will be held from time to time in our official Discord community, and we will continue to push these forward while looking to try more forms of community co-creation content. Everyone is welcome to follow along and actively participate!

> ***Q48***
> By extension of this question, do you have any plans on directly sponsoring any content creators — whether it be Streamers or Youtubers?

***A48***
First, I would like to extend a special and heartfelt thank you to THPDom for his video, "Why I fell in love with this card-based gacha game." In it, he shared that Morimens gave him a sense of courage when facing the concept of death — this resonates perfectly with the core themes we strive to convey, and I was truly moved to tears when I watched it.

It was also seeing him read the dialogue from the last section of the Final Chapter aloud in full that led me to make up my mind to allocate additional voiceover budget for that segment. His work has also brought many dedicated new players to Morimens.

Since then, I have begun to seriously think about whether we should collaborate with more content creators to help the community continue to grow. We are currently still exploring more suitable and sustainable ways to make this happen.

> ***Q49***
> There's been hints that a few collaborations might be on the way. Can we get any hints on whether these will be characters, or strictly narrative events? And will they be mutual or one sided collaborations?

***A49***
The forms of collaboration are not limited to any single format: there may be purely narrative events like Morimens × My Turn, or one-way character crossovers like Morimens × Mist Sequence. Looking ahead, we certainly aren't ruling out two-way collaborations either.

In terms of the collaboration cycle, we will adhere to the principle of "no one-time content": collaboration-related content will be presented in a way that allows for future opportunities to obtain it again, rather than being permanently missed if you weren't there at the time. After all, the theme of Morimens is "using memory to fight against Dissolution" — if collaboration content were unable to remain in the game in some form long-term, it would truly contradict the narrative spirit of Morimens.

Of course, based on the realities of the industry and licensing, we can typically only sign collaboration agreements with a term of several years, allowing unlimited reruns within that period and renewing upon expiration. 

If a license eventually expires and cannot be renewed due to objective reasons, we will inform everyone well in advance and make the appropriate arrangements and contingency plans.

As for the upcoming collaboration: it will be a one-way collaboration, and the other party is a classic work of profound influence with a distinct cosmic horror flavor. This will be a special character crossover.

> ***Q50***
> And now for a hard question. As I stated in the introduction of this interview, Morimens exists within the Gacha Space — a slice of gaming that is infamous for predatorial relationships with their player base. Nonetheless, I've so far found the game to be rather generous with players, so I felt it prudent to ask. 
> 
> What sort of measures do you put in place in order to alleviate that sort of dynamic with your players, or otherwise try to avoid the worst elements otherwise associated with what Gacha typically is? And, do you feel that Brain In A Vat's current approach towards monetization is something you're comfortable with — or are there measures you'd like to implement to make it more consumer friendly?

***A50***
The gacha model certainly has a history of being misused, and I don't intend to defend the model itself. I can only explain the specific choices B.I.A.V. Studio made within this framework, and why.

Based on commercial realities, the scale of the work the team wanted to achieve, and the choice to target the mobile market, the first producer chose the gacha model at the very start of development in 2019. However, since our testing and launch in 2023, the system designers have worked hard to "smooth out" the parts of this model most likely to provoke conflict and anxiety, making it more transparent, restrained, and sustainable. In terms of drop rates, pity rules, package value, and in-game reward distribution, we try to be relatively more player-friendly than other titles in the genre.

 At launch, the game also provided monthly exchangeable universal special materials that could be used to enhance characters in place of duplicate pulls — meaning that players willing to invest time could gradually develop their favorite characters to a higher level of completion. We even reduced the hard pity count during the first year, making it easier for everyone to obtain characters. 

On the gameplay side, the game also provides free auto-recovering revival items, and during the first year we removed the achievement requirement in Normal Mode that prohibited the use of Emergency Gnosis, allowing players to use these mechanics more freely to reduce pressure. On the reward design side, we have also worked to minimize the anxiety of "miss it and it's gone forever": even limited rewards will typically become obtainable again through other means after some time.

However, we don't believe the current situation is perfect. Early on, in order to preserve monetization space, Enlighten and Wheel of Destiny were designed with a relatively high duplicate pull requirement, while concentrating key mechanical benefits in the first three Enlighten levels or stacks.

 While this is friendly to most players, it also led a small number of players who wanted to focus on specific characters to feel that their investment was high but the sense of return was insufficient. Data also shows that overall willingness to pay has been lower than expected, and feedback that "the number of duplicates required for full development is too high" has been quite concentrated. Facing real commercial and operational needs, the game later had no choice but to gradually introduce Over-Exaltation, additional level caps, and the Final Axiom — content that will continue to be added incrementally in the future. 

While these further enhancements to the existing progression line did bring improvements in monetization, they also sparked controversy and created a certain psychological burden for players.When facing the contradiction between needing to sustain commercial operations and not wanting to place too much pressure on players, we genuinely handled it poorly due to insufficient experience and understanding.

If we could do it over again, we would have thought through these trade-offs more thoroughly before launch: appropriately reducing the number of duplicate requirements, and striking a more reasonable balance on drop rates and pity, to reduce the unexpected anxiety caused by repeated mid-operation adjustments. Returning to the present — since we must be cautious about making core structural changes to the monetization and progression systems, the planned direction going forward is: increasing free benefits and event rewards with every major version; providing more opportunities to obtain previously limited content; launching more convenient, personalized, and stress-reducing features; and continuing to optimize overflow rewards for duplicate characters.

We have always hoped that through continuous iteration, the game as a whole will become more and more player-friendly.

> ***Q51***
> Small aside — FOMO is very much an element built into many Gacha's, though, I've yet to encounter any instances of true FOMO yet related to core elements to the game, given there absence of any true limited characters or wheels — even in the case of the collab character. That said, the existence of the timed Echo'd Pilgrimages galled me a little (possibly because I'm broke as hell, and incorporating a very tight timer on deals puts a rush on a player). 
> 
> Is that a monetization mechanism that's here to stay, or will there be more pushes to implement things like the longer last event bundles like the Panlong deal, or given their association with the games chapters — bundling them with the altogether generous 'Chronicles'? (And, is there any chance that the unused/activated Echo'd pilgrimages might become accessible to players who weren't able to take advantage of them for some reason or another — in order to mitigate FOMO pressure, and allow for more flexibility insofar as to fully invest in the game).

***A51***
The fear of missing out is undeniably an extremely effective design strategy. Whether it's useful gameplay content, beautiful visuals, or even very ordinary rewards, simply being labeled as "permanently unavailable" will significantly heighten players' urge to acquire them. All team members are players themselves — and because development keeps us so busy, we aren't always able to keep up with the games we love in time, so we suffer just as much from the anxiety of things becoming permanently unavailable.

That said, Morimens cannot completely avoid this common practice either — there will still be some "limited-time" content in the game, such as limited-time banners, seasonal memorabilia, event-exclusive Wheels of Destiny, and event-produced Phantasmal Attire. 

But as you have observed, there is almost no truly "permanently unavailable" core content in the game: this type of limited-time content will typically rerun after some time, or be made available through other means — though the cost may be higher, or the wait longer.

As for time-limited event packages, there was indeed a persistent FOMO problem in the past. However, we have confirmed that future updates will provide a way to reopen previously expired unpurchased packages — at reasonable intervals and under reasonable conditions — in order to further minimize this anxiety to the lowest possible level.

> ***Q52***
> And speaking of investments — You've gone on the record to say that when Morimens eventually reaches a point of End Of Service, you've every intention of creating a single player version so people can enjoy the story and Mechanics — something which I can definitely appreciate as someone heavily invested in video game preservation. That said, would this also be open towards new players, or more towards honoring the investment original players placed into the game? 
> 
> Moreover, how exactly do you forsee the mechanics converting from its current form to a more single player oriented one — given the existence of the pull system, as well as things like the energy system? Will it be fairly accessible to earn characters and their dupes in the single player experience you envision — and would things like the costumes still exist, or would they be more of a relic for older players?

***A52***
We wteant the single-player version of Morimensto preserve, as much as possible, players' completion progress, account progression, cosmetic items, achievements, unlocked content in the Premium Gallery, and other unlocked content.

However, we don't want this to be just a "commemorative album" for veteran players. Some games have brought offline versions to players after shutting down, and I was deeply inspired by this — for example, Taro Yoko's SINoALICE. We hope to go a step further on that foundation, transforming it into a complete work that can still be seriously played and is open to new players as well.

On the technical side, we plan to embed a "virtual private server" into the single-player version to simulate locally the core logic that originally relied on servers. The single-player version will cut certain features that require an online connection, and will provide a way to transfer account data to the local client within a certain period of time. 

Because the data will be converted to local storage, the single-player version will no longer support cross-platform interoperability; at the same time, it will also support normally creating new local accounts to play. As for the questions you raised about how the gacha, stamina acquisition, and item obtainability will be adapted — and whether things will be easier to obtain — considering the need to prioritize respecting everyone's historical investment during the live-service phase, these settings will not change significantly at that time.

I know this promise is easy to question. Since the time for this day to arrive is still far off, we have not yet finalized all the legal and technical details. 

But one thing the team is already doing is maintaining the separability of the architecture as much as possible during development, so that when the time comes, the engineering work required for an offline version won't be so large as to be unachievable. And I will always reserve a budget for fulfilling this promise. This is the most honest answer I can give right now.

> ***Q53***
> Lastly, if you can maintain a healthy degree of traction for Morimens, do you intend to continue expanding its universe for as long as you can — or do you have an endpoint and mind? And if there is an endpoint, do you have any projects in mind as a follow up, whether in the gacha space, or otherwise?

***A53***
If that is the case, we will of course continue to expand the world of Morimens! While the Finale of the First Arc significantly accelerated the narrative pace and reached the phased endpoint originally planned for the game, we still have many ideas for continuing to enrich this world; and for now, we have not set a new endpoint for the story.

If someday in the future Morimens develops well enough, and the team truly has brand new themes to explore and experiences to create, I will seriously consider launching subsequent new projects — whether or not they take the form of a gacha game or an entirely different genre.

At this stage, we all hope to pour as much energy as possible into Morimens, striving to develop it into a long-term IP that can transcend the lifecycle of a single game. This goal may be ambitious, but B.I.A.V. Studio will see it through, one step at a time.

> ***Thanks***
> 
> Anyways, that's all my questions, so I want to offer my utmost thanks for offering me the opportunity to pick your mind and ask all these questions. It's been a pleasure finding out all of these bits and bobs, and just engaging with the game and general — and I look forward to potentially picking your brain again in the future.

Light: Your questions were very in-depth, and many of them touched on the core issues that Morimens has repeatedly grappled with throughout its development and operations.

Being able to look back through this exchange on the choices and reflections the team has made along the way, and to articulate our future direction more clearly, has been very valuable to us. Thank you so much for the interview and your questions!
